video_title: The Old Guard 2 | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Charlize delivers, the rest needs to catch up
The Old Guard 2 understood the value of its leading lady. Seeing Charlize Theron in every action scene, unobscured by stunts or cheap tricks, is absolutely the film's strong point, setting it apart from other generic and forgettable productions. Charlize is convincing as an immortal warrior because her body is on display—kicking, punching, fighting with the precision and elegance of a true martial artist. The choreography has also evolved, abandoning (thankfully!) the exhaustively recycled "John Wick-style" formula, in which the character hits furniture more than enemies. In Old Guard 2, we see striking, high kicks, and hand-to-hand combat that excites because it finally feels like fighting—not simulations edited to look like choreography. But not everything survived the editing room unscathed. The choice to speed up the footage and exaggerate the cuts weakens precisely the film's most valuable asset: the actress's physique and the credibility of the action. The excessive cuts are reminiscent of generic Marvel scenes, where no one knows who's fighting, where the blow came from, or why the camera is turning. If there's a path for the next sequence, it's clear: keep Charlize in focus, without unnecessary cuts. Preserve the natural speed of her movements—as you did masterfully in Atomic Blonde—and let her talent breathe. The audience will notice, and be grateful. You have a star capable of delivering real action, and a universe where that can shine. Just don't sabotage it with impatient editing or formulas from other films. If you're bold enough, The Old Guard could become a benchmark—and not just another forgettable franchise.

comment_text:
My review of "The Old Guard 2" over at IMDB.  The headline is "Was this written by an 8-year-old?"


Utter trippe (which rhymes with sh***e, a very useful English word in this case).

Looked cheap (terrible effects).  Made the characters stupid beyond words.  Everyone looked old and tired.  

But the STUPID IT BURNS!  The first film set up their "powers" pretty simply, in that it just stated they existed, and eventually there was an expiration of some kind.  And that was it, and they could move on with the story, which was fun enough as it went.  It was even clever in that these people were effectively immortal but not superhuman.  

The sequel?  All of a sudden, there's "more" revealed.  There's an immortal no one knew about who's been living like a monk, studying these people.  Then there's another immortal, supposedly the oldest of the immortals.   Meanwhile, the Immortal Monk all of sudden knows there was a "legend" that explains how powers can be taken away from the immortals and how it can magically be given to someone else just by wishing it or something.  But who would have known that?  The immortals apparently didn't know it, and who would have told this legend?

There was a lot more of that, a lot more of "let's pull another convenient explanation out of our creative constipated nethers because we aren't willing (or smart enough) to do the work to create a logical reason for any of this nonsense."

And, for the love of all that is cinematically holy, having scripts written by people who's only experience in life is video games.  I mean, once the team is the final setting, chasing the Big Boss, stuff just happens.  There are bombs that the heroes just happen to set off and take them out of the action.  There are frozen nitrogen traps that the good guys just happen to be standing under that will take them out of the action.  And there are convenient stairs and hallways leading to different dangers everywhere.  Just like playing Tomb Raider on an old PS-2 or something.

Finally, during the showdown between Andy, the heroine, and the Big Bad, there are helos doing slow, threatening flybys of our Andy.  She needs to stop the helos.  She's a 3000-year-old-warrior, can use modern weapons with tremendous proficiency.  She has a two headed axe in her hands when a helo cruises by, level with her and about twenty feet away, and she should know that hurling the axe into the helo blades who take that thing down.

Does she do it?  Is a Bear Catholic?

Oy, so much dumb, like, the newest member of the team discovering a new double bladed spear, and with mere weeks of practice she is taking on the fearsome warrior from the past who has about a millennia or two of practice with bladed weapons...and the New Chick is magically holding her own with the Warrior, and not being sliced into sushi.

Viewers should sue Netflix and the "writers" for stealing their time.

comment_text: very disappointed in this sequel. As someone who was a HUGE fan of the first one, I was quite surprised at how poorly this was written and made. It was not emotionally engaging, there was no real hook, and there was no real central story. Just a brief touch on many arcs at the same time. It made it impossible to feel anything for any of the arcs. I am genuinely disappointed in this sequel. So much so that this is the first time in my life I am ever leaving a review for a movie. Creating is not an easy task, especially when you've set a high bar for yourself, so i'll give those behind the movie kudos. But for a movie that I can sum up all the arcs in three sentences, I think this was a terrible sequel. So much wasted potential.
